headline: more AI, less stable
sub: Google DORA 2024: a 25% jump in AI adoption, measured. The cause was batch size, not code quality.

headline: the model isn't the variable. your codebase is.
sub: Stanford, 100k+ devs across 600+ companies. One legacy codebase saw 2.5x more rework.

headline: agency vs in-house is the wrong question

cols.head: WHO BUILT IT
cols.body: swappable. agencies, hires, contractors come and go.

cols.head: WHAT IT RUNS ON
cols.body: the operating layer: context, orchestration, review gates.

headline: a learning system with no memory isn't learning

cols.head: NO CANONICAL LAYER
cols.body: each agent keeps its own scratch, re-derives, drifts.

cols.head: ONE READ/WRITE SOURCE
cols.body: the correction lands once. every agent reads it.

headline: rag isn't memory
sub: Retrieval ranks a stale doc right next to a live one. Memory is a write-back source of truth.

headline: a demo and prod are different sports
sub: A demo runs once, clean, watched. Prod runs on your mess, unattended, every day.

headline: rag isn't memory
sub: Similarity has no notion of current. A stale chunk ranks right next to a fresh one.

headline: more agents never meant more speed
sub: They just stepped on each other faster. Shared memory plus atomic claims fixed it: ~60% fewer tokens.

headline: a fleet only improves with one place it writes back to
sub: No shared source of truth, no learning. Just N agents repeating the same mistake in parallel.

headline: buying a copilot is the easy part
sub: You can't purchase the rewire: where context lives, who reviews the output, how a task gets claimed.

headline: a CLAUDE.md older than your last refactor is a map of a place that no longer exists
sub: Regenerate it from the repo, or your agent navigates by a map of the past.

headline: orchestration is the easy part
sub: The 80% is the operating layer: shared memory, atomic claims, a fleet view.
A fat window buries the part that mattered, and the agent pays for all of it on every call.

headline: the agents that earn their keep are the boring ones
sub: A clear job, and a place to write back. That's the whole trick.

headline: augment, never replace
sub: The fleet made the humans sharper, not redundant.

headline: two agents kept undoing each other
sub: We wired one shared memory they both read and write back to. Fixed.
My run on our repos, first real day. Not a universal claim; I quote ~60% publicly.

headline: the cheapest token is the one your agent never re-reads
sub: Serve one canonical doc per query instead of re-reading the repo. ~60% fewer tokens.
